- You’re taking the bar to the chest and locking out each rep—nice full range of motion.
- Head, upper back and glutes look in contact with the bench most of the set.

b) Main fixes
- Elbow angle: Your elbows look a bit flared near the bottom (bar lands a little high on the chest). Drop the elbows to roughly a 45-degree angle to your torso on the descent to keep shoulders happier and the bar path consistent.
- Upper‑back setup: You appear fairly flat. Plant your upper back hard into the bench and adduct (pinch) your shoulder blades to create a stable platform. Keep those four points of contact: head, upper back, glutes, and feet.
- Feet and leg drive: Your heels look lifted and the feet a bit loose. Keep your feet planted (either toes or flat is fine) and don’t let them wander. Push your knees out slightly and squeeze your glutes to stay tight—no “floppy feet.”
- Bar path consistency: Pick a fixed spot on the ceiling, touch the chest without bouncing, then drive the bar back toward that exact spot. This removes drift toward the uprights and keeps reps identical.
- Grip/wrist tightness: Crush the bar and “bend the bar” to engage the upper back. Keep wrists stacked over elbows to avoid soft wrists.
- Unrack: Lift the bar “out, not up” so you don’t lose your upper‑back tightness. Near heavier efforts, use a spotter to help with the liftoff.

c) Mental cues
- Feet planted, knees out, squeeze glutes.
- Pinch shoulder blades; puff the chest.
- 45° elbows on the way down.
- Eyes on one ceiling spot; press back to it.
- Grip hard and “bend the bar.”

{"answer": "Love the outdoor grind—let’s dial this in for more power and chest activation.

What looks good
- Smooth, controlled reps through a solid range.
- Your butt stays on the bench—good start to a stable base.

Main fixes
- Foot position/leg drive: Your feet are set wide and forward and you drift onto the toes. Plant your heels flat, pull your feet back as far as you can while keeping them flat, and keep the legs closer to the bench when viewed from the front. This creates a firm base for leg drive.
- Upper back tightness: Your shoulders look like they “unpack” as you press. The upper back should be planted firmly against the bench with the shoulder blades adducted (pinched together) and depressed the whole set. Use the back as a platform to drive against.
- Elbow path: You’re flaring quite a bit. On the descent, drop the elbows down and to the side at roughly a 45-degree angle rather than straight out—this keeps stress off the shoulders and lets the pecs and triceps do the work.
- Stay still during the press: Keep knees, hips, and shoulder blades motionless on the concentric. Avoid shrugging the shoulders to start the rep.

Mental cues
- Heels down, feet back, legs tight to the bench.
- Pinch shoulder blades and glue them to the pad; keep your chest up.
- 45° elbows on the way down; drive straight back up.
- Stare at one spot on the ceiling and press to that same spot every rep.

The first thing I want to talk about is foot placement. While not as crucial as on squat or deadlift, it still is important on the bench press. It forms your connection to the ground and your base. I prefer to keep a flat foot on the ground. This ensures I get the best connection possible. I also like to keep my back tight and arched. To do that, I like to keep my feet back as far as I can while still keeping them flat. For everyone, this is going to be a different position, but you want to put them back, but not so far back that you can't keep them flat on the floor.

- Upper back tightness: Your shoulders look like they “unpack” as you press. The upper back should be planted firmly against the bench with the shoulder blades adducted (pinched together) and depressed the whole set. Use the back as a platform to drive against.
- Elbow path: You’re flaring quite a bit. On the descent, drop the elbows down and to the side at roughly a 45-degree angle rather than straight out—this keeps stress off the shoulders and lets the pecs and triceps do the work.
- Stay still during the press: Keep knees, hips, and shoulder blades motionless on the concentric. Avoid shrugging the shoulders to start the rep.

Mental cues
- Heels down, feet back, legs tight to the bench.
- Pinch shoulder blades and glue them to the pad; keep your chest up.
- 45° elbows on the way down; drive straight back up.
- Stare at one spot on the ceiling and press to that same spot every rep.

- You’ve got a solid arch and your upper back looks engaged against the bench.

- Drive the heels down and forward—don’t let the butt float.
- Squeeze and bend the bar.

{"answer": "Awesome work getting full-range bench reps on video—let’s dial in a few details to make them stronger and safer.

What looks good
- You’re taking the bar to the chest each rep—nice full ROM.
- The bar speed looks controlled (not wildly bouncing).

Main fixes
- Wrist and grip: The bar is sitting high in your hands and your wrists are extended back. Set the bar in the heel of your palm directly over the forearm bones, knuckles facing the ceiling, and grip hard. This stacks the bar over the forearm and reduces energy leaks.
- Forearm angle/grip width: At the bottom your forearms don’t look perfectly vertical. Adjust grip width so that when the bar touches your chest, forearms are straight up-and-down.
- Elbow path: Your elbows appear a bit flared. During the descent, drop the elbows to roughly 45 degrees relative to your torso to keep shoulders happy and the bar path efficient.
- Upper-back setup: Pinch your shoulder blades together and keep your upper back planted on the bench as a solid platform. Maintain four points of contact the whole set: head, upper back, glutes, and feet.
- Unrack and bar path relative to the hooks: You’re very close to the uprights and risk clipping them. Set up with eyes under the bar, unrack by lifting out (not up), move the bar to a lockout position directly over your shoulder joints, then lower to the chest and drive it back to that exact spot—an upside-down J path.
- Feet and leg drive: Your heels/feet look unstable. Either plant your feet flat or get solidly on your toes, but keep them fixed. Squeeze your glutes and drive your legs into the floor as you press, keeping your butt on the bench.
- Touch control: Lightly touch the chest each rep without bouncing so your ROM stays consistent.

Mental cues
- Knuckles to the ceiling; bar in the heel of the palm.
- Pinch shoulder blades; chest up; “bend the bar.”
- Eyes locked on one spot on the ceiling; send the bar back to that spot.
- Elbows ~45° on the way down; forearms vertical at the chest.
- Grip hard, squeeze glutes, feet planted—drive the floor.

If you can, grab a quick side view next time so I can confirm bar path and elbow angle precisely.", "context": "While there are exceptions, doing squats for greater than 10 reps or so will usually cause your cardiovascular system to fatigue before your leg musculature, leading to sloppy form, worse strength gains, and lower muscle activation. Now generally, I recommend loading the squat with at least seventy percent of your one-rep max or in the five to ten rep zone if your goal is primarily hypertrophy, and the three to six rep zone if your goal is primarily strength. And again, there will be exceptions to this. {"inputs": {"user_query": "are there any issues with my form?"}, "outputs": {"answer": "Awesome work getting full-range bench reps on video—let’s dial in a few details to make them stronger and safer.

What looks good
- You’re taking the bar to the chest each rep—nice full ROM.
- The bar speed looks controlled (not wildly bouncing).

Main fixes
- Wrist and grip: The bar is sitting high in your hands and your wrists are extended back. Set the bar in the heel of your palm directly over the forearm bones, knuckles facing the ceiling, and grip hard. This stacks the bar over the forearm and reduces energy leaks.
- Forearm angle/grip width: At the bottom your forearms don’t look perfectly vertical. Adjust grip width so that when the bar touches your chest, forearms are straight up-and-down.
- Elbow path: Your elbows appear a bit flared. During the descent, drop the elbows to roughly 45 degrees relative to your torso to keep shoulders happy and the bar path efficient.
- Upper-back setup: Pinch your shoulder blades together and keep your upper back planted on the bench as a solid platform. Maintain four points of contact the whole set: head, upper back, glutes, and feet.
- Unrack and bar path relative to the hooks: You’re very close to the uprights and risk clipping them. Set up with eyes under the bar, unrack by lifting out (not up), move the bar to a lockout position directly over your shoulder joints, then lower to the chest and drive it back to that exact spot—an upside-down J path.
- Feet and leg drive: Your heels/feet look unstable. Either plant your feet flat or get solidly on your toes, but keep them fixed. Squeeze your glutes and drive your legs into the floor as you press, keeping your butt on the bench.
- Touch control: Lightly touch the chest each rep without bouncing so your ROM stays consistent.

Mental cues
- Knuckles to the ceiling; bar in the heel of the palm.
- Pinch shoulder blades; chest up; “bend the bar.”
- Eyes locked on one spot on the ceiling; send the bar back to that spot.
- Elbows ~45° on the way down; forearms vertical at the chest.
- Grip hard, squeeze glutes, feet planted—drive the floor.

{"answer": "Love the effort here—solid, steady reps!

a) What looks good
- You’re keeping your butt and upper back on the bench the whole time.
- Controlled touch to the chest each rep—nice.

b) Main fixes
- Foot/leg drive: Your feet are set forward with shins angled, which makes leg drive hard. Bring your feet back so shins are nearly vertical and plant the heels. Keep the legs close to the bench and use the floor to reinforce your arch.
- Upper‑back platform: Get tighter by pinching your shoulder blades together and down before the unrack so your upper back is a solid platform to press from.
- Eye position and bar path: Lock your eyes on one point on the ceiling. Touch the lower chest, then “throw” the bar back toward that ceiling point so it follows an upside‑down J path instead of straight up over your chest.
- Elbow angle: From this angle it looks a bit tucked at the bottom. Aim for roughly a 45° angle between your upper arm and torso on the descent.
- Grip/wrist stack: Your wrists appear slightly extended. Grip harder and keep the bar over the base of your palm so the wrist stacks directly over the forearm at lockout.

c) Mental cues
- Heels down, knees out, push the floor.
- Pinch shoulder blades into the bench; chest high.
- Crush the bar and “bend it.”
- Eyes fixed on one ceiling spot; touch low chest, then throw the bar back to that spot.
- Four points planted: head, upper back, glutes, feet.

- Rack position: You’re pressing very close to the uprights and could clip the j‑hooks. Slide yourself slightly down the bench so your eyes start directly under the bar and the bar is around forehead height. Unrack by lifting out, not up.
- Upper‑back setup: Your shoulders look a bit loose/protracted. Plant your upper back and pinch your shoulder blades together to create a solid platform.
- Elbow angle/bar touch: Your elbows flare wide and the bar touches high on the chest. Drop the elbows to ~45° relative to your torso and let the touch point settle lower on the chest. Think “tuck” on the way down.
- Grip and wrists: Your wrists appear bent back and it looks like a thumbless grip. If you’re newer to the lift, wrap the thumbs. If you keep thumbless, make sure the bar sits stacked over the wrist joint (not back toward the fingers) and squeeze hard.
- Bracing and leg drive: Get your feet set as far back as comfortably possible with heels planted (hips down on the pad). Take a big breath and brace your abs before each rep, and keep steady leg drive throughout.

c) Mental cues
- Eyes fixed on one ceiling spot; drive the bar back to it.
- Pinch shoulder blades; drive your back up into the bar.
- Bend the bar into a U / protect your armpits (tuck to ~45°).
- Stack wrists over the bar and crush the grip.
- Heels down, knees out, squeeze glutes—stay tight.
